section_01_metadata.filing_delay_note: Board approval 7.5 months after year-end. Auditor sign-off 8 months after year-end. Significant delay — signals operational stress.
section_01_metadata.auditor_change_note: KPMG audited 2015. EY now in place by 2021. Auditor change between crisis years is a governance signal worth noting.
section_01_metadata.registered_office: 1 Ziguinchor Street, Off IBB Way, Wuse Zone 4, Abuja
section_01_metadata.regulatory_framework: MYTO (Multi-Year Tariff Order), NERC, EPSRA 2005, TEM Rules (effective 1 February 2015)

section_02_auditor_opinion.opinion_type: Unmodified (clean) with Material Uncertainty — Going Concern
section_02_auditor_opinion.going_concern_paragraph: EY drew attention to net loss of ₦11.95 billion for 2021 and net current liabilities of ₦112.43 billion. Ability to continue as going concern dependent on actualizing budgeted results and receiving additional FGN/CBN support.

key_audit_matters.matter: Recognition of customer-contributed assets relating to prior periods
key_audit_matters.description: Transformers and other assets donated by customers in prior years had been omitted from fixed asset register. Corrected via IAS 8 restatement. Affected 2019, 2020 and opening 2020 balance sheet.
key_audit_matters.vitics_significance: Customers were physically building the grid with their own money — and AEDC wasn't even recording it. This is structural underfunding made invisible in accounting.

key_audit_matters.description: Impairment allowance of ₦88.72 billion against gross trade receivables of ₦139.49 billion — representing 63.6% of gross trade receivables. High judgment required in ECL model inputs.
key_audit_matters.vitics_significance: Nearly two-thirds of all bills ever raised are considered uncollectable. The billing system is largely fictitious. Consumers are being billed for electricity they often didn't receive or can't afford.

prior_year_restatement.reason: Customer-contributed assets (transformers, substations) omitted from books in 2019 and earlier

revenue_from_contracts_with_customers.note: Core electricity sales revenue. Massive jump of 71% — driven by Service Reflective Tariff (SRT) full-year implementation.

tariff_shortfall_awarded_by_NERC.note: Non-cash revenue — regulatory subsidy netting off NBET payables. Not cash received from consumers. Declined sharply in 2021 vs 2020.

customer_contributed_assets.note: Assets physically donated to AEDC by consumers — meters, transformers, substations. Customers continuing to self-fund the grid. Declined from peak 2020 level.

section_03_profit_and_loss.consecutive_loss_years: At least 7 consecutive years of losses (2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019 loss, 2020 loss, 2021 loss — 2019 was anomalous profit from large tariff shortfall recognition)
section_03_profit_and_loss.total_comprehensive_income_note: Despite ₦11.95 billion loss, total comprehensive income is positive ₦42.36 billion due to ₦54.1 billion revaluation surplus on distribution network assets. Balance sheet looks healthier than operating reality.

section_04_balance_sheet.equity_prior_year_note: Equity was negative ₦2.08 billion at end of 2020. Now positive ₦40.29 billion in 2021 — entirely due to ₦54.1 billion network revaluation, NOT business improvement.

five_year_retained_losses_trend.note: 2019 anomaly due to large tariff shortfall recognition (₦102bn). Underlying losses are structural and continuous.

section_05_cashflow.operating_cashflow_swing_note: Dramatic swing from +₦13.3 billion operating cash in 2020 to -₦7.8 billion in 2021. Driven by ₦30.45 billion increase in trade receivables.
section_05_cashflow.financing_note: Net positive financing entirely from ₦16.53 billion new CBN loan drawdown. Company dependent on government liquidity injections to survive.
section_05_cashflow.investing_note: Net positive due to ₦7.45 billion government grant receipt (NMMP infrastructure). Actual capex only ₦6.07 billion.
section_05_cashflow.capex_note: Actual cash capex only ₦6.07 billion on a ₦185 billion asset base — maintenance ratio of ~3.3%. Gross underinvestment in a deteriorating grid.
section_05_cashflow.restricted_cash_note: ₦10.19 billion cash collateral for UBA letter of credit for NBET participation. ₦9.32 billion set aside for CBN VAT remittance policy. Total ₦19.5 billion is locked — real free cash is much lower than headline ₦13.3 billion.

section_06_revenue_analysis.tariff_regime: Service Reflective Tariff (SRT) — introduced late 2020, first full-year implementation in 2021
section_06_revenue_analysis.SRT_impact: Core electricity revenue jumped 71% from ₦76.0bn to ₦129.9bn — entirely due to tariff increase, not more electricity delivered

revenue_by_payment_mode.note: 65% of consumers still on postpaid — mostly unmetered, estimated billing. Prepaid growth is the metering story.

section_06_revenue_analysis.unbilled_revenue_note: ₦7.57 billion of revenue 'earned' in December 2021 but billed in January 2022. Revenue recognition timing inflates 2021 numbers.
section_06_revenue_analysis.tariff_shortfall_mechanism: NERC awards tariff shortfall for gap between cost-reflective tariff and actual approved tariff. Settled via netting off NBET payables — not cash received.
section_06_revenue_analysis.tariff_shortfall_decline_note: Shortfall recognition declining as SRT brings tariffs closer to cost-reflective levels. But NBET debt continues to accumulate.

CBN_NEMSF_NMMP_loan.drawdown_breakdown: ₦9.8bn to settle NBET market debts, ₦6bn to settle MAP/NMMP meter providers, ₦8.1bn disbursed directly to Company
CBN_NEMSF_NMMP_loan.note: CBN loan is the primary life-support mechanism. Company cannot operate without continuous government liquidity injection.

TCN_loan.purpose: Establish letter of credit for NBET TEM participation

NELMCO_loan.purpose: Letter of credit for NBET TEM participation

CAPMI_loan.note: Consumer advance payments for meters — customers pre-paid for meters, AEDC owes repayment. As at year end all outstanding balance repayable on demand.

NBET_ONEM_payables.payment_required: Payable on demand per NERC orders
NBET_ONEM_payables.restructuring_discussion: Management seeking to convert to 20-year term loan. No approval obtained as at report date.
NBET_ONEM_payables.going_concern_note: This ₦124 billion is due immediately but FGN/NERC have effectively deferred calling it. If called, Company collapses instantly.

deferred_income_government_grant.note: Day-one gain arises because CBN loans are at below-market rates. Difference between fair value and face value is recognized as deferred income — accounting gain, not real income.

unmetered_low_tier_postpaid.note: The worst segment — ₦112 billion of bills owed by unmetered, low-income, postpaid customers with 69% loss rate. This is the estimated billing problem. These are the most vulnerable consumers.

government_agencies.note: Government itself is a chronic bad debtor. ₦12.8 billion owed by MDAs with 44% loss rate.

prepaid_customers.note: High loss rate for PPM appears to relate to legacy debts from customers who migrated from postpaid to prepaid. Not current prepaid default.

section_08_receivables_and_collections.collection_crisis_summary: AEDC billed ₦139.5 billion cumulatively to customers but has given up on ₦88.7 billion of it — 64 cents of every naira billed is written off as uncollectable. The grid serves customers who cannot or will not pay. This is the core structural failure.
section_08_receivables_and_collections.super_vendor_note: AEDC paid ₦1.68 billion in commissions to digital payment vendors (Kallak Power, UBA, Pagatech) for enabling prepaid vending. Transition to prepaid has real cost.

section_09_metering.metering_programme: National Mass Metering Programme (NMMP) — NERC initiative, Phase 0
section_09_metering.MAP_regulation: Meter Asset Provider (MAP) Regulation — 3 MAPs appointed in 2019
section_09_metering.NMMP_note: ₦6 billion of 2021 CBN loan disbursement used to settle MAP meter providers. Government financing consumer metering.
section_09_metering.consumer_meter_contributions_note: ₦0.81bn in 2021 vs ₦5.95bn in 2020 — sharp decline. MAP/NMMP programme is replacing CAPMI consumer self-funding model.
section_09_metering.CAPMI_note: ₦1.12 billion still owed to consumers who advanced meter money under CAPMI scheme. All repayable on demand.
section_09_metering.metering_gap_status: Persistent. Despite NMMP and MAP, majority of customers (65%) remain on postpaid/unmetered estimated billing in 2021.
section_09_metering.metering_vitics_signal: Metering gap is the bridge to solar adoption. Unmetered consumers facing estimated bills are the most likely to defect to solar when they can afford it — they get no benefit from paying bills for electricity they dispute.

section_10_tariff_and_regulatory.tariff_regime_2021: Service Reflective Tariff (SRT) — full-year first implementation
section_10_tariff_and_regulatory.SRT_introduction: Introduced by NERC in late 2020. Bands customers by hours of supply received.
section_10_tariff_and_regulatory.MYTO_framework: Multi-Year Tariff Order (MYTO) 2015, with minor reviews. MYTO 2022 order issued (NERC/295/2021) — key milestone for going concern.

tariff_shortfall_history.note: Total accumulated tariff shortfalls are a measure of how far actual tariffs were below cost-reflective levels. ₦102bn for just 4 years (2015-2018).

section_10_tariff_and_regulatory.minimum_remittance_increase_significance: NERC forcing DISCOs to pay more of electricity cost through to NBET. Reduces AEDC cash available for operations. Pressure intensifying.
section_10_tariff_and_regulatory.cost_reflective_tariff_status: Not yet fully achieved. Going concern assumption requires full cost-reflective tariff implementation in near future.

regulatory_fine.note: AEDC contested regulatory fine in court. ₦361 million total provision for fines and penalties.

section_11_grid_defection_signals.grid_defection_score: 5/10 — Conditions for mass defection fully formed by 2021
section_11_grid_defection_signals.scoring_rationale: 2021 is the year conditions crystallized. SRT tariff increase made electricity visibly more expensive. Estimated billing crisis worsening. NMMP metering not reaching scale fast enough. Solar content on YouTube Nigeria was already searchable at scale (2017+). Government solar visibility high post-Solar Power Naija 2020.

signal_1_tariff_shock.description: Full-year SRT implementation caused 71% jump in core electricity revenue — consumers faced dramatically higher bills in 2021
signal_1_tariff_shock.consumer_impact: For unmetered consumers, estimated bills increased sharply. For metered consumers, tariff bands meant new costs tied to unreliable supply hours.
signal_1_tariff_shock.solar_connection: Tariff shock is the #1 documented driver of solar adoption research in Nigeria. 2021 begins the pre-watershed buildup.

signal_2_collection_collapse.description: ₦88.7 billion in uncollectable bills — 63.6% of all billings written off
signal_2_collection_collapse.consumer_impact: Consumers defaulting at scale. Those who cannot pay are the same cohort that later finds solar financing more manageable than grid bills.
signal_2_collection_collapse.trend: Impairment charge up 81% year-on-year — crisis accelerating

signal_3_persistent_metering_gap.description: 65% of customers still on postpaid/estimated billing despite NMMP
signal_3_persistent_metering_gap.consumer_impact: Unmetered consumers have no accountability, no trust. They are being billed for electricity they dispute. Solar removes this arbitrary billing entirely.
signal_3_persistent_metering_gap.solar_connection: Prepaid solar removes estimated billing — one of the top stated reasons for consumer solar interest in Nigeria

signal_4_supply_unreliability_proxy.description: Service Reflective Tariff banding implies AEDC customers receive differentiated hours. Some bands receive very few hours — these consumers get the worst value from grid.
signal_4_supply_unreliability_proxy.note: AEDC does not publish ATC&C loss rates or hours-of-supply data in this report. Absence of this data is itself a signal.

signal_5_revaluation_as_mask.description: ₦54.1 billion revaluation of distribution network assets turned negative equity to positive in 2021. Balance sheet looks stronger — but operational reality is unchanged.
signal_5_revaluation_as_mask.vitics_insight: A company that needs asset revaluation to avoid negative equity is not a company consumers can trust to reliably maintain their grid. Asset revaluation is a financial engineering response to operational failure.

section_11_grid_defection_signals.energy_sales_vs_customers_note: 2021 report does not provide customer count vs kWh delivered comparison. This is the key solar defection metric. Absence prevents definitive grid defection scoring — but trajectory signals indicate rapid approach to 2023 watershed.

section_12_consumer_impact.scoring_rationale: 2021 delivered the worst combination yet: higher bills (SRT), no metering improvement for majority, persistent supply unreliability, and a system openly acknowledged as requiring government life-support to survive. The consumer received more financial burden with no guaranteed improvement in service.
section_12_consumer_impact.tariff_impact: First full year of Service Reflective Tariff. Core revenue up 71% on no increase in electricity delivered — 100% of increase is tariff, not supply.
section_12_consumer_impact.billing_injustice: ₦88.7 billion in uncollectable bills means the billing system is producing numbers disconnected from consumer ability or willingness to pay. Estimated billing is at crisis level.

consumer_as_asset_donor.assets_contributed_cumulatively: Customers have contributed transformers, substations, meters — restated into books in 2021. Consumers funding infrastructure that a privatised DISCO should be funding.

section_12_consumer_impact.CAPMI_exposure: ₦1.12 billion owed to consumers who advanced money for meters — all repayable on demand. Consumer money locked inside a company with going concern risk.
section_12_consumer_impact.government_agencies_as_bad_debtors: ₦12.8 billion owed by government MDAs with 44% loss rate. The state that consumers pay taxes to is also not paying its electricity bills.
consumer_vocabulary_expected: no light but they still bill me
I don carry solar leave AEDC

section_13_five_year_trend.revenue_trend_note: 2019 spike is entirely tariff shortfall recognition (₦102bn+). Not real consumer payment. 2021 growth is real — driven by SRT tariff increase.
section_13_five_year_trend.loss_trend_note: 2019 profit anomaly from one-off tariff shortfall recognition. Underlying losses are structural and continuous across all years.
section_13_five_year_trend.net_assets_trend_note: Net assets turned positive in 2021 due entirely to ₦54bn network revaluation. Without revaluation, equity would be approximately -₦14bn.
section_13_five_year_trend.PPE_growth_note: ₦51 billion jump in PPE from 2020 to 2021 — almost entirely revaluation (₦54bn) not real new infrastructure investment (capex only ₦6bn).
section_13_five_year_trend.vitics_five_year_summary: Seven years after privatisation (2013-2021), AEDC has: never made a sustainable operating profit, accumulated ₦58.9bn in retained losses, required ₦48.8bn in CBN emergency loans, billed consumers for ₦139.5bn they cannot collect, and asked consumers to donate ₦17.3bn+ in physical infrastructure. The privatisation promise of efficiency, investment and better service has not been delivered.

force_majeure_history.2015_event: KANN declared force majeure in April 2015 (withdrawn February 2016). Not repeated in 2021 report.
force_majeure_history.2021_status: No force majeure declared. However going concern uncertainty is a functional equivalent — AEDC cannot discharge obligations without continuous government intervention.
force_majeure_history.regulatory_fine_contested: Company contested regulatory fine (₦250 million) in court — not resolved as at report date.
force_majeure_history.COVID_relief_note: CBN COVID relief interest rate of 5% (vs normal 9%) applied to new 2021 loans until March 2023. COVID context shaped financing terms.

cbn_intervention_fund.facility_name: Nigerian Electricity Market Stabilisation Facility (NEMSF) + National Mass Metering Programme (NMMP)
cbn_intervention_fund.vitics_signal: ₦48.8 billion in CBN emergency loans since 2018 is the clearest signal that the privatised DISCO model has failed to become financially self-sustaining. Consumer electricity bills cannot cover costs. Government is subsidising electricity distribution through financial engineering while consumers simultaneously subsidise through donated assets and unpaid estimated bills.

nelmco_transition_liabilities.nature: All pre-completion receivables and liabilities transferred to NELMCO at privatisation
nelmco_transition_liabilities.pre_completion_receivables: All trade receivables as at 31 October 2013 transferred to NELMCO without recourse
nelmco_transition_liabilities.pre_completion_liabilities: All liabilities and contingent liabilities as at 31 October 2013 transferred to NELMCO
nelmco_transition_liabilities.reconciliation_status: RECONCILIATION STILL NOT COMPLETED AS AT 2022 (9 years post-privatisation)
nelmco_transition_liabilities.vitics_signal: Nine years after privatisation, AEDC and NELMCO still haven't reconciled what was transferred. This is the transition liability that keeps lingering — consumers live inside an unresolved handover. The legal and financial architecture of the privatisation is incomplete.

customer_contributed_assets.prior_period_restatement: Transformers contributed in 2019 and earlier were not recorded. Corrected in 2021 via IAS 8 restatement — ₦13.3bn added to 2020 opening balance.
customer_contributed_assets.accounting_treatment: Recognised at fair value as PPE. Corresponding deferred income released as revenue when performance obligation discharged.
customer_contributed_assets.cumulative_significance: Over multiple years, consumers have funded transformers, substations and metering infrastructure that should be AEDC's capital expenditure. This is an extraordinary transfer of burden from privatised utility to consumers.
customer_contributed_assets.CAPMI_note: ₦1.12 billion owed by AEDC to consumers who pre-funded meters under CAPMI — all repayable on demand. Consumer as creditor to a going-concern company.
customer_contributed_assets.vitics_signal: When consumers fund the infrastructure of the company that bills them, and that company still can't achieve cost-reflective tariffs or positive equity, the consumer incentive to exit the system entirely (solar) becomes rational, not just aspirational.

section_18_vitics_analysis.document_position_in_transition_timeline: AEDC 2021 is the penultimate pre-watershed document. Two years before the 2023 fuel subsidy removal and mass solar adoption surge.

key_vitics_findings.finding: SRT tariff shock without supply improvement = solar push factor
key_vitics_findings.detail: First full-year SRT caused 71% revenue jump from tariff alone. Consumers paying significantly more without receiving more electricity. This is the documented push factor that precedes solar interest spikes in YouTube search data.

key_vitics_findings.finding: Collection collapse signals inability to pay = pre-solar financial profile
key_vitics_findings.detail: 63.6% impairment rate means most consumers cannot or will not pay grid bills. These consumers are the same cohort that solar financing companies later target with pay-as-you-go and instalment models. Collection failure IS the solar market pipeline.

Consumers donating transformers and substations to AEDC while paying estimated bills creates a specific resentment profile. By 2021, a decade of this pattern has created consumers acutely aware they are subsidising a failing system.

key_vitics_findings.finding: Revaluation creates false confidence — reality unchanged
key_vitics_findings.detail: ₦54bn network revaluation makes the 2021 balance sheet look healthier than 2020. But cash operations deteriorated (operating cash: +₦13.3bn in 2020 vs -₦7.8bn in 2021). Misleading headline numbers may mask urgency of reform from policymakers.

key_vitics_findings.finding: CBN loans as permanent subsidy confirms tariff inadequacy
key_vitics_findings.detail: ₦48.8bn in CBN loans since 2018. Each drawdown confirms that the tariff charged to consumers is structurally insufficient to cover costs. Yet those same insufficient tariffs are increasing annually. Consumer cost is rising; service reliability is not.

key_vitics_findings.finding: Board change December 2021 signals shareholder escalation
key_vitics_findings.detail: Entire old board exited 12 December 2021. New board installed. Managing Director changed post-year-end (April 2022). Multiple leadership changes signal CEC Africa Investments (ultimate parent) recognising the model is in crisis.

direct_terms: AEDC prepaid meter how to get
indirect_solar_transition_terms: how to stop paying AEDC bills

how_claude_should_read_this_document.primary_lens: Did AEDC 2021 change anything for the Nigerian energy consumer — and what did consumers say about it?
how_claude_should_read_this_document.answer: No. 2021 intensified consumer burden (SRT tariff increase) without delivering reliability improvement. The collection crisis confirms consumers rejected the bargain. The consumer response — in YouTube search terms — would show rising solar interest directly correlated with the SRT introduction.
how_claude_should_read_this_document.connection_to_2023_watershed: AEDC 2021 shows the kindling fully assembled: higher bills, unmetered majority, infrastructure donated by consumers, ₦88.7bn in uncollectable debts. The 2023 fuel subsidy removal was not the cause of solar adoption — it was the match dropped into kindling that AEDC and other DISCOs had been building for a decade.
how_claude_should_read_this_document.connection_to_2015_report: Compare to AEDC 2015: in 2015 impairment was ₦18.3bn, in 2021 it is ₦88.7bn — 4.8x increase in 6 years. Revenue up but uncollectable receivables up proportionally more. The crisis is not being solved; it is being deferred and expanded.
how_claude_should_read_this_document.sdg_7_relevance: AEDC 2021 provides zero evidence of progress toward SDG 7 (affordable and clean energy access). Tariffs are rising, reliability is unproven, metering gap persists, and the DISCO's going concern depends on government support — not on serving consumers sustainably.

section_18_vitics_analysis.transition_note: AEDC 2021 is a transition marker — it represents the final year of pre-watershed conditions. The SRT, the collection crisis, the metering gap, the CBN dependency, and the board crisis are all visible here. When solar adoption surged in 2023, AEDC 2021 will have been the last financial portrait of the grid before the exodus began.

section_19_document_quality.document_type: Full audited annual financial statements including notes
section_19_document_quality.readability: High — typed digital PDF, not scanned. All numbers extractable with high confidence.
section_19_document_quality.completeness: Complete. Includes: Directors Report, Statement of Directors Responsibilities, Independent Auditor Report (EY), P&L, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow, Statement of Changes in Equity, all Notes (1-35), Value Added Statement, Five-Year Financial Summary.
limitations: No hours-of-supply data by band (key grid defection metric)
No ATC&C loss rates published (hidden metric)
No customer count trend data — prevents direct solar defection calculation
Going concern basis means all numbers carry uncertainty about future viability
section_19_document_quality.extraction_method: Full visual and textual reading of all 79 pages
section_19_document_quality.extracted_by: Claude Sonnet 4.6 for Vitics — Nigeria AI Energy Intelligence Platform
